question: How do I group a DataFrame by date and aggregate values per group?
ideal_answer: Use df.groupby(df['date'].dt.floor('D')).agg(...) — or pd.Grouper(key='date', freq='D') inside groupby — and pass a dict or named aggregation. The groupby user-guide section describes named aggregations and the freq aliases like 'D', 'M', 'W'.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/groupby.rst (aggregate section) + docs:doc/source/user_guide/timeseries.rst (multi-agg). Top retrieval candidates were a mix of issues + docs; kept the docs hits.

question: What is the difference between .loc and .iloc when selecting rows?
ideal_answer: .loc selects by label (index value or boolean mask); .iloc selects by integer position. Mixing the two raises IndexingError; recommended split is .loc for label-based, .iloc for positional.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/getting_started/intro_tutorials/03_subset_data.rst + canonical maintainer reply on issues:28548.

question: What does the SettingWithCopyWarning mean and how do I fix it?
ideal_answer: Chained indexing creates an ambiguous view-or-copy; resolve with a single-step .loc[mask, col] = value assignment. The indexing user guide has a dedicated section on returning-a-view-versus-a-copy.
notes: PLACEHOLDER — filled in T027. Target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/indexing.rst (view-vs-copy section).

question: How do I resample a time-series to monthly frequency?
ideal_answer: Use df.resample('MS') for month-start or 'ME' (formerly 'M') for month-end, then chain mean/sum/agg. The timeseries user guide covers resample and the freq aliases.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/timeseries.rst + intro_tutorials/09_timeseries.rst + 10min.rst.

question: How do I read a CSV file with a non-comma separator and a custom NA marker?
ideal_answer: pd.read_csv(path, sep=';', na_values=['N/A','-']). Both sep and na_values are documented under the IO Tools page.
notes: target: issues:26448 (canonical 'use sep=";"') + issues:20877 (read_csv docs link) + issues:10647 (NA values round-trip).

question: How do I create a MultiIndex DataFrame from a list of tuples?
ideal_answer: pd.MultiIndex.from_tuples([...], names=[...]) then pass it as index or columns to DataFrame. See the advanced-indexing user guide.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/dsintro.rst (tuples-dict) + two issue examples showing from_tuples + names.

question: How do I handle timezone conversion when joining two DataFrames whose timestamps are tz-naive on one side and tz-aware on the other?
ideal_answer: Convert both sides to tz-aware UTC first (.dt.tz_localize('UTC') for tz-naive, .dt.tz_convert('UTC') for tz-aware) before merging. The timeseries user guide covers tz_localize/tz_convert.
notes: PLACEHOLDER — filled in T027. Target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/timeseries.rst (time-zone handling section).

question: How do I drop rows that contain any missing values?
ideal_answer: df.dropna() drops rows with any NA. Pass how='all' to drop only fully-NA rows, or thresh=N to require N non-NA values. See missing-data user guide.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/10min.rst (DataFrame.dropna example). Retrieval was issue-heavy for this query; the single docs hit is the canonical answer.

question: What's the difference between merge, join, and concat?
ideal_answer: concat stacks DataFrames along an axis; merge does SQL-style joins on keys; join is a DataFrame method that wraps merge on the index. See the merging user guide.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/merging.rst (concat anchor). The discriminator parents for merge/join sit in the same file; max-aggregation picks the highest-scoring.

question: How do I convert a string column to the categorical dtype?
ideal_answer: df['col'] = df['col'].astype('category'). Categorical can also be constructed with pd.Categorical(values, categories=..., ordered=...). See categorical user guide.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/categorical.rst + docs:doc/source/user_guide/io.rst (Specifying categorical dtype + parsed Categorical columns).

question: When should I use apply versus map versus applymap?
ideal_answer: Series.map is element-wise on a Series; DataFrame.applymap is element-wise on a DataFrame (deprecated in favour of DataFrame.map); DataFrame.apply applies a function along an axis. See basics user guide.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/user_defined_functions.rst (map vectorization caveat) + docs:doc/source/whatsnew/v2.1.0.rst (Styler.applymap deprecation).

question: Why does df.append no longer exist and what should I use instead?
ideal_answer: DataFrame.append was deprecated and removed; use pd.concat([df, other], ignore_index=True). The whatsnew page records the removal; the merging guide covers concat patterns.
notes: PLACEHOLDER — filled in T027. Target: docs:doc/source/whatsnew + docs:doc/source/user_guide/merging.rst.

question: How do I write extension dtypes (e.g. Int64, string) that handle missing data without converting to float?
ideal_answer: Use the nullable integer dtype 'Int64' (capital I) or 'string' dtype — both back NA with pd.NA instead of NaN. See integer-na user guide and the v0.24.0 whatsnew on Int64 ExtensionDtype.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/integer_na.rst (IntegerArray) + two v0.24.0 whatsnew parents on Int64 ExtensionDtype + nullable integer dtype.

question: How do I plot a DataFrame as a line chart with multiple columns on the same axes?
ideal_answer: df.plot(kind='line') plots every numeric column against the index by default; pass y=[...] to subset. See visualization user guide.
notes: target: visualization user guide. Retrieval surfaced issues:14136 (subplots) and issues:29460 (df.plot(x, y=[...], kind='line')) — kept those as the canonical answer pattern.

question: How do I compute a rolling mean over a 7-day window on time-series data?
ideal_answer: df.rolling(window='7D').mean() — pass a string offset to use a time-based window. See window/rolling user guide.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/window.rst (time-based window example) + v0.18.0 whatsnew (rolling freq='D').

question: How do I contribute a bug fix to pandas — what is the dev workflow?
ideal_answer: Fork on GitHub, create a feature branch, set up the dev environment per CONTRIBUTING, run pytest, open a PR. The contributing guide on the docs site is the canonical reference; maintainers consistently link new contributors to it.
notes: target: CONTRIBUTING.md does not seem to be in the corpus parents as a single chunk; canonical answer surfaces as maintainer replies pointing at the contributing guide (issues:21527, issues:12554).

question: Why does groupby().agg() return a DataFrame with a MultiIndex on the columns when I pass a list of aggregations?
ideal_answer: When you pass a list of aggregation functions, the resulting DataFrame has a MultiIndex on columns of (column, agg_name). Pass a dict-of-lists or named aggregation to flatten. The groupby user guide and the v0.20.0 whatsnew on .groupby(..).agg(..) syntax cover this.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/groupby.rst (aggregate.agg anchor) + docs:doc/source/whatsnew/v0.20.0.rst (agg syntax) + issues:573 (canonical maintainer Q&A on MultiIndex-after-agg).

question: Why do I see a dtype change to float64 after I read a CSV with integer columns that contain missing values?
ideal_answer: Standard int dtypes can't hold NaN, so pandas upcasts to float. Use dtype='Int64' (nullable integer) when reading to preserve integer semantics with NA.
notes: target: issues:20407 (read_csv inferring float64 when it should be uint64 — the canonical user surprise) + issues:16569 (dtypes-in-read_csv FAQ).

question: Why is set_index slow on a large DataFrame and what's the recommended workaround?
ideal_answer: set_index can copy the underlying array and triggers a sort if the key is non-monotonic; the standard workaround is to pre-sort the key column with sort_values(...) and then call set_index. Maintainers also commonly point at drop=False to avoid the column drop.
notes: PLACEHOLDER — filled in T027. Target: maintainer replies on issues about set_index perf (issues:26064, issues:26182, issues:11056 are top retrieval candidates).

question: How does pandas decide when to copy versus when to return a view, and why does SettingWithCopyWarning appear?
ideal_answer: Chained indexing (df[mask][col] = ...) creates a temporary intermediate that may or may not be a view. The recommended fix is the single-step .loc assignment df.loc[mask, col] = ...; see the indexing user guide's 'returning a view versus copy' section.
notes: target: canonical maintainer replies pointing at the user-guide view-versus-copy anchor (issues:6023, issues:24798, issues:42703).

question: How can I pivot a long-form DataFrame into wide-form with multiple value columns?
ideal_answer: df.pivot(index=..., columns=..., values=[...]) accepts a list of value columns; the result has a MultiIndex on columns. See reshaping user guide.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/reshaping.rst (pivot example) + docs:doc/source/getting_started/intro_tutorials/07_reshape_table_layout.rst.

question: What is the recommended way to iterate over rows of a DataFrame, and why is iterrows discouraged?
ideal_answer: iterrows returns (index, Series) pairs and is slow because each row is materialized into a Series — and dtypes get coerced to object. Prefer vectorized operations, itertuples for read-only access, or apply/transform for per-row computation.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/basics.rst — the iteration section's intro + iterrows description + itertuples-preserves-dtypes paragraph all surfaced cleanly.

question: How do I sort a DataFrame by multiple columns with different sort orders per column?
ideal_answer: df.sort_values(by=['a','b'], ascending=[True, False]) — both lists must be the same length. See basics user guide / sort_values.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/whatsnew/v0.9.1.rst (per-column ascending) + docs:doc/source/user_guide/basics.rst (MultiIndex sorting).

question: How do I read a multi-sheet Excel file and merge the sheets on a common key?
ideal_answer: pd.read_excel(path, sheet_name=None) returns a dict of DataFrames keyed by sheet name; iterate and pd.merge them on the shared key column. See IO Tools / Excel.
notes: PLACEHOLDER — filled in T027. Target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/io.rst (Excel section).

question: How can I detect and drop duplicate rows in a DataFrame, and what does keep='last' do?
ideal_answer: df.duplicated() flags duplicate rows after the first; df.drop_duplicates() removes them. keep='first' (default) keeps the first occurrence; keep='last' keeps the last; keep=False drops every duplicate including the original.
notes: target: docs:doc/source/user_guide/indexing.rst — three adjacent parents around the duplicate-rows section.
